entry.title: Cross Deployment Unit Issue – Cross-DU Issue: Auto Create ServiceRequest on Prospect Creation
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I have a requirement in SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C) and need guidance on the correct architectural approach.

Business Requirement:

When a Prospect (Customer – standard BO) is created and the Role Code is BUP002, a ServiceRequest (standard BO) should be created automatically. The ServiceRequest must have a specific Processing Type, a predefined Subject (e.g., “Verification Ticket”), and should be linked to the Prospect.

System Landscape:

Customer BO (standard) belongs to the Foundation Deployment Unit.

ServiceRequest BO (standard) belongs to the Service/CRM Deployment Unit.

Problem:

When I try to use ServiceRequest.Create() inside Customer.xbo (AfterModify), I receive the error:

"Access of member 'Create' in a cross-deployment unit is not possible."

Since both are standard BOs in different Deployment Units, direct creation is not allowed.

My Questions:

If both objects are standard BOs, can IC (Integration Component) be used directly between them?

For this scenario, how many Custom BOs are required?

Is one Custom BO in the Service DU enough (as a bridge)?

Or are two Custom BOs required (one in each DU)?

If using a Custom BO in Service DU, how can it be triggered when a Prospect is created in Customer BO, considering cross-DU restrictions still apply?

What is the SAP-recommended best practice for this type of cross-deployment requirement? Should this be implemented using:

Custom BO bridge pattern

Workflow rules

Web Service / OData

Or another approach?

I would like to follow a clean and SAP-compliant design without violating cross-deployment restrictions.

Any guidance on the correct architecture would be appreciated.

Thank you.

C4C Service  C4C Service  SAP CRM, GWI client plug-in SAP Cloud Applications Studio

entry.title: Re: Impact of choosing "standard acct" in document splitt...
entry.content:
Hi @luxman_shankar ,

Yes, using a “constant profit center” as the fallback in SAP’s standard account assignment is a common and fully supported practice, but it comes with some design considerations that are worth thinking through before you implement it across multiple company codes.

If your organization has one controlling area, has centralized finance/CO reporting and wants simple error handling, A single constant profit center is absolutely standard and widely used.

If your organization has multiple company codes with different reporting owners and needs audit traceability per entity and wants cleaner segmentation of derivation failures, then consider company‑code‑specific fallback profit centers via custom derivation.

entry.title: Re: Impact of choosing "standard acct" in document splitt...
entry.content:
Hello @luxman_shankar ,

Using a single fallback profit center for multiple company codes in document splitting is technically supported and commonly used, but it should be carefully monitored to avoid distortions in profit center reporting.

If you choose a constant fallback profit center:

-Ensure it is valid across all company codes.
-Use it only for exception handling.
-Implement monitoring (e.g., periodic report on postings with that profit center).

Many implementations also create a naming convention such as:
PC9999 – Default / Derivation Error Profit Center, so finance teams can easily identify and correct these postings.

 

Question / ScenarioExplanation, Best Practice & SAP Notes
What does “Standard Account Assignment” mean in document splitting?In document splitting configuration, Standard Account Assignment is used as a fallback value when the system cannot derive a splitting characteristic (e.g., Profit Center, Segment). The system assigns the predefined constant value to ensure the document remains balanced.
Using a constant Profit Center when derivation failsYes, this is a common approach. When the system cannot determine the profit center from the source object (cost center, WBS, asset, etc.), the fallback profit center ensures that the document splitting process does not stop with an error.
Scope of the configurationThis setting is client-wide, meaning the same fallback profit center will be applied across all company codes if derivation fails.
Concern: assigning the same fallback profit center for multiple company codesThis is technically allowed and commonly used, especially in global templates. However, you must ensure that the fallback profit center exists and is valid for all company codes where it may be used.
Accounting impactIf the fallback profit center is triggered frequently, financial statements by profit center may become distorted because postings are assigned to the default profit center instead of the correct operational one. Therefore, it should only be used as an exception.
Recommended best practice✔ Use fallback profit center only as a technical safeguard. ✔ Monitor postings hitting the fallback profit center regularly. ✔ Investigate root causes for missing derivations (master data gaps, missing substitutions, incorrect account assignments).
Alternative design approachSome companies create a dedicated “Error / Suspense Profit Center” per company code or per controlling area to better monitor incorrect derivations.
MonitoringBuild reporting to track postings using the fallback profit center so configuration or master data can be corrected.
SAP Help ReferenceSAP Help → Document Splitting in New General Ledger SAP Help → Profit Center Derivation and Balancing in Universal Journal
Relevant SAP Notes (Verify Applicability)📌 SAP Note 891144 – Document Splitting: Standard Account Assignment Logic 📌 SAP Note 1155429 – Profit Center Derivation in Document Splitting 📌 SAP Note 1939127 – Balancing Fields in Document Splitting 📌 SAP Note 2344012 – Document Splitting Behavior in S/4HANA

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: WHT (Withholding Tax) input when create PR (Purchase...
entry.content:
Hello @ailliyuni ,

For automatic WHT calculation during MIRO:

-Use Extended Withholding Tax configuration as primary mechanism
-Implement BAdI MRM_WT_SPLIT_UPDATE if additional derivation logic is required
-Avoid using BAPI or GGB0 for WHT calculation logic

Flow:

PO → MIRO → BAdI reads business rules → populate WHT type/code → SAP calculates WHT.

Use BAdI: MRM_WT_SPLIT_UPDATE

 

Requirement / QuestionExplanation, Technical Options & SAP Notes
Requirement: automatic WHT calculation when parking/posting invoice (MIR7 / MIRO)In standard SAP, Extended Withholding Tax (EWT) calculates WHT automatically during MIRO posting based on vendor master configuration and withholding tax setup.
Does SAP support line-item WHT calculation in MIRO?Yes. When Extended Withholding Tax is active, SAP calculates WHT at document level or line-item level depending on configuration of withholding tax type and base amount definition.
Where WHT logic is normally determinedWHT calculation uses: • Vendor master data (LFBW) • Company code WHT configuration • Withholding tax type and code • Base amount (invoice line items)
If additional logic is requiredIf business logic requires deriving WHT based on PO attributes, material group, cost center, etc., enhancement is required during MIRO processing.
Recommended BAdI for WHT manipulationBAdI MRM_WT_SPLIT_UPDATE allows modification or creation of withholding tax lines during MIRO processing. This BAdI is specifically designed for WHT adjustments in Logistics Invoice Verification.
Other possible enhancement spots• BAdI MRM_HEADER_DEFAULT – default header values • BAdI MRM_ITEM_DEFAULT – manipulate invoice item values before posting
Can BAPI be used for this?❌ BAPI is not recommended for controlling WHT logic inside MIRO because invoice verification processing uses internal structures. Enhancements must be implemented using BAdI or exits.
Can Validation (GGB0) determine WHT?❌ GGB0 validation can only check conditions and raise messages. It cannot determine or calculate withholding tax values dynamically.
Typical enhancement logic1️⃣ During MIRO item processing read PO reference 2️⃣ Determine WHT type/code based on business rule 3️⃣ Update WHT structure via MRM_WT_SPLIT_UPDATE 4️⃣ Allow SAP to perform standard WHT calculation
Data tables involvedVendor WHT setup stored in LFBW. Calculated WHT amounts stored in accounting document tables (BSEG / ACDOCA equivalent structures).
SAP Help ReferenceSAP Help → Extended Withholding Tax in Accounts Payable SAP Help → Logistics Invoice Verification Enhancements
Relevant SAP Notes (Verify Applicability)📌 SAP Note 830717 – Extended Withholding Tax Determination 📌 SAP Note 1156325 – Withholding Tax Processing in Invoice Verification 📌 SAP Note 1548013 – WHT Handling in MM Invoice Verification 📌 SAP Note 2220152 – Extended Withholding Tax in S/4HANA

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Timesheet Time Entry with Multiple Activity Allocation & Activity Derivation Functionality
entry.content:
Hello dear SAP Gurus,

   In our customer we need an overhead allocation functionality with activity types to post project related indirect costs from cost center to WBS elements. The design expectation is using activity types sourced by timesheet, since costing sheet is not flexible and determination of overhead rates can not be done because of less correlation with any base account group, it is off the table.  Let me make our scenario clearer :

-> Project planning held and source demand took place,

-> A worker post timesheet entry and activity T001 (Labor) has allocated, journal entry created via 943* -> 100 EUR credits CC, debits WBS

Because our client would want to observe general project costs and deprecitation ( which are not going to run onto WBS' but CC's) during the period ( at least close to the actual expectation as possible via cost rates) and costing sheet isn't functional for them, they would also like to observe at least two other activities from a single timesheet entry; lets say T002 (Depr.) and T003 (GPC) 

The same can be managed on work center level on acitivity confirmation scnearios in production, yet as far as we understand that timesheet entries have 1:1 relations with activity types which are determined by the demand and the worker service cost level. Also, template allocation functions are not available in Cloud Public edition, we can't manage to derivate multiple activity allocations from a template. 

My question is, is there such functionality of multiple activity allocation from single timesheet entry supported in standard ? If not, is there a tool to derivate multiple activity allocations with reference to source entry ? 

And in general, is there another procedure under this scope to allocate overheads from cost centers to WBS elements ? Briefly, expectation is tracking activity allocations in projects similar to production.

   Thanks in advance for your guidance and support, please ask if you need more details on the requested info.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition SAP Timesheet SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Finance

entry.title: EAM_TASKLIST_CREATE – “Service package required” error when creating multiple task lists sequentiall
entry.content:
I am trying to create multiple maintenance task lists programmatically using EAM_TASKLIST_CREATE in ABAP. 

Scenario:

  • Two maint. plans are in a loop and for each i am executing EAM_TASKLIST_CREATE, POST and COMMIT.  
  • Creating two maintenance task lists sequentially in the same program run. 
  • Both contain service operations. 
  • Each plan works correctly when executed separately.

Problem:

  • When executed one after another in the same program, the second task list fails during POST with error:

" Operation ... requires a service package! ".  But this operation does not need a service package.

  • Debugging shows that in the method GET_SERVICE_PACKAGE , the variables are:

my_ext_header-packno = 0000397416    
lv_packno = 0000397313

This causes an early RETURN and service data is not filled.  my_ext_header-packno should be initial or the same like lv_packno for the service data to be filled. packno = 0000397416 is from the first plan.

What I have tried:

  • Clearing all internal tables (gt_opr_ins, gt_spack_lines, etc.) before second creation.

  • Executing BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT after the first creation.

  • Using DESTINATION 'NONE' in the function call.

  • Wrapping the call in a separate RFC-enabled wrapper.

  • Verified that gt_opr_ins-packno = 0000000000 for both plans.

  • Both plans work individually without errors.

Questions:

  • What is the recommended way to programmatically create multiple task lists with service operations in one run?

entry.title: Re: GR Cancellation/reversal issue: "Deficit of PU GR qua...
entry.content:
Why the error occurs
Your PO line has invoices posted greater than the received quantity. When you try to cancel a GR (movement type 102 or 122), the system would reduce the received quantity further. That would make the situation even worse: invoiced > received by an even larger margin. SAP blocks this with the “Deficit of PU IR quantity” error to ensure consistency between GR and invoice verification (GR‑based IV).

How to Resolve
You cannot bypass this check with OMJJ/OMBZ alone — those settings only control tolerance and message behavior. The root cause is the invoice quantity > GR quantity imbalance. To fix:

1) Reverse the Invoice First
Use MR8M (Cancel Invoice Document) or MRIS (Settle Invoices) depending on your process. This reduces the invoiced quantity so that GR cancellation becomes possible.

2) Then Reverse the GR.
Once the invoice is canceled, you can post the GR reversal (102 or 122). The system will allow it because invoiced ≤ received.

3) Alternative: Adjust via Credit Memo
If you cannot cancel the invoice (e.g., already paid), post a credit memo against the PO to bring the invoiced quantity back in line. After that, the GR reversal can be processed.

entry.title: Seeking Configuration Points for Colombia DRC (F350, Income Tax, and VAT) - S/4HANA Cloud Public Edi
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I am currently working on a localized implementation for Colombia using SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.

While navigating the Central Business Configuration (CBC) and Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC), I have successfully identified the configuration points and SSCUIs required to link the "Información Exógena" formats (DIAN 1001 to 1009).

However, I am having trouble locating the specific configuration nodes or SSCUIs for the following statutory reports:

  1. Formato 350 (Monthly Withholding Tax Declaration).

  2. Income Tax Declaration (Declaración de Renta).

  3. VAT Declaration (IVA - Formulario 300).

I have already explored the Tax Box Framework (SSCUIs 103011, 103012, and 103013), but it is not clear if these specific forms follow the same mapping logic as the Exógena formats or if they require a different setup within the "Setting Up Your Statutory Reporting" (102220) activity for this specific release.

Could anyone point me toward the correct configuration path or provide the specific SSCUI IDs for these forms in the 2602 version?

Thanks in advance for your help!

Best regards,

Juan Sebastian Valencia SAP Consultant | Structum Ingeniería S.A.

entry.title: How to access Withholding Tax Summaries and Cumulative Reports in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community experts,

We are currently working on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 with the Colombia Localization active. We need to identify the standard Fiori applications or reports to visualize a detailed breakdown and cumulative summary of withholding taxes (Income Tax, VAT, and ICA) by tax indicator for a specific period.

Specifically, we are looking for a view that provides:

  1. Cumulative values grouped by Withholding Tax Type and Indicator.

  2. Transactional details including Journal Entries (Asientos contables), Company Code (Sociedad), and Base Amounts.

  3. Traceability from the summary down to the specific accounting document.

We have explored the Manage Withholding Tax Items (F5428) app, but it seems to be missing from our Launchpad even when searching by ID.

Our questions are:

  • What is the exact Business Role and Business Catalog required in release 2602 to access withholding tax summaries for Colombia?

  • Is the app F5428 the standard tool for this version, or has it been replaced/integrated into the Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC) framework (e.g., via Run Statutory Reports - F1515)?

  • Are there any specific SSCUI configurations (like activating the Withholding Tax Reporting Date - 105376) that are mandatory to make these records visible in the reporting apps?

Any guidance on the "Best Practice" path to get a reliable tax list for reconciliation before legal submission would be greatly appreciated.

Best regards, Juan Sebastian Valencia

entry.title: SAP Build Code – Technical Series – SAP Continuous Integration and Delivery
entry.content:
Guide to CI/CD in SAP Build Code

Introduction

Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) is a crucial practice in modern software development, enabling teams to deliver high-quality code faster and more reliably. SAP Build Code provides a robust CI/CD service that integrates seamlessly with your development workflow, automating the build, test, and deployment processes.

In this guide, we'll walk through setting up and configuring a complete CI/CD pipeline for a SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) application. We'll cover everything from basic setup to advanced configurations, with real examples and screenshots at every step.

What You'll Learn

  • Setting up your first CI/CD pipeline in SAP Build Code - CI/CD Service
  • Advanced job configuration with repository-based config
  • Automating builds with webhooks and triggers
  • Implementing multi-branch strategies for feature development
  • Integrating SAP CI/CD build status with GitHub Pull Requests

Prerequisites

Before we begin, ensure you have:

  • SAP BTP account with access to SAP Continuous Integration & Delivery service
  • Git repository (In this blog GitHub is going to be used)
  • Basic knowledge of CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model)
  • Node.js and npm installed locally
  • @sap/cds-dk installed

Our Sample Application

For this guide, we'll use a simple CAP application with a "Hello World" service endpoint. While minimal, this application demonstrates all the key CI/CD concepts without unnecessary complexity.

Repository structure:

cicd-service/
├── .sap_cid/
│   └── config.yaml              # CI/CD pipeline configuration
├── srv/
│   ├── hello-service.cds       # Service definition
│   └── hello-service.js        # Service implementation
├── tests/
│   └── hello-service.test.js   # Unit tests
├── jest.config.js              # Test configuration
├── mta.yaml                    # Deployment descriptor
├── package.json                # Dependencies
└── xs-security.json            # Security config

---

Part 1: Setting Up Your First CI/CD Pipeline

Step 1.1: Create Your CAP Application

First, let's look at our simple CAP application structure.

Service Definition (srv/hello-service.cds):

service HelloService {
   function sayHello() returns String;
}

Service Implementation (srv/hello-service.js):

module.exports = (srv) => {
   srv.on('sayHello', () => { return 'Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD!'; 
   });
};

This minimal service provides a single function that returns a greeting message.

Step 1.2: Add Unit Tests

Quality assurance starts with testing. We'll use Jest to test our service.

Test File (tests/hello-service.test.js):

const cds = require('@sap/cds/lib');
const path = require('path');

describe('Hello Service', () => {
    let srv;

    beforeAll(async () => {
        // Load the CDS model
        const csn = await cds.load(path.join(__dirname, '../srv/hello-service.cds'));

        // Deploy the model to an in-memory database
        await cds.deploy(csn);

        // Get the service
        srv = await cds.serve('HelloService').from(csn);
    });

    test('sayHello returns correct message', async () => {
        const result = await srv.tx().send('sayHello');
        expect(result).toBe('Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD!');
    });
});

Test locally:

npm install
npm test

You should see:

PASS  tests/hello-service.test.js
  Hello Service
    ✓ sayHello returns correct message (4 ms)

------------------|---------|----------|---------|---------|-------------------
File              | % Stmts | % Branch | % Funcs | % Lines | Uncovered Line #s
------------------|---------|----------|---------|---------|-------------------
All files         |     100 |      100 |     100 |     100 |
 hello-service.js |     100 |      100 |     100 |     100 |
------------------|---------|----------|---------|---------|-------------------

Step 1.3: Initialize Git Repository

# Initialize Git
git init

# Add all files
git add .

# Create initial commit
git commit -m "Initial commit: Simple CAP application for CI/CD demonstration"

Step 1.4: Push to GitHub

Create a new repository on GitHub (without initializing it), then:

# Add remote (replace YOUR-USERNAME with your GitHub username)
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/YOUR-REPO.git

# Push to GitHub
git push -u origin main

Screenshot 1.1: GitHub repository overview showing all files (.sap_cid will appear later on the exercise).

Step 1.5: Access SAP Continuous Integration & Delivery

  1. Open your SAP BTP Cockpit
  2. Navigate to your subaccount
  3. Go to Services → Instances and Subscriptions
  4. Find Continuous Integration & Delivery
  5. Click to open the service

Screenshot 1.2: SAP CI/CD service in BTP Cockpit

Step 1.6: Add Your Repository

In the SAP CI/CD service:

  1. Click Repositories in the top menu
  2. Click + (Add) button
  3. Configure your repository:
  • Name: cicd-service
  • Clone URL: Your GitHub repository URL
  • Credentials: Add your GitHub credentials (I have used a Personal Access Token in this case)

Screenshot 1.3: Adding repository to SAP CI/CD

Step 1.7: Create Your First Job

Now let's create a CI/CD job using the Job Editor (GUI-based configuration):

  1. Click Jobs in the top bar.
  2. Click + (Create Job)
  3. Configure the job:
  • Job Name: cicd-service-main
  • Repository: Select cicd-service
  • Branch: main
  • Pipeline: Select Cloud Foundry Environment

Screenshot 1.5: Creating a new job in Job Editor

Step 1.8: Configure Build Stage

In the Stages tab, configure the Build stage:

  • Build Tool: mta
  • Build Tool Version: Java 21 Node 22
  • Build Descriptor: mta.yaml

Screenshot 1.6: Build stage configuration

Step 1.9: Configure Additional Unit Tests Stage

Enable and configure the Additional Unit Tests stage:

  • npm Script: test
  • Build Descriptor: package.json

Screenshot 1.7: Test stage configuration

Step 1.10: Save and Run Your First Build

  1. Click Create to save the job
  2. Click Run to trigger your first build
  3. Watch the pipeline execute!

Screenshot 1.9: Build successful with all stages passed

Click on any stage to view detailed logs.

 Congratulations! You've successfully set up your first CI/CD pipeline in SAP Build Code!

---

Part 2: Advanced Job Configuration with Source Repository Config

So far, we've used the Job Editor (GUI) to configure our pipeline. While user-friendly, it has limitations for complex configurations. Let's explore Source Repository Configuration mode, which uses a configuration file in your repository for more flexibility and version control.

2.1: Why Use Source Repository Configuration?

Benefits:

  • Configuration as code - version controlled with your application
  • More advanced options and customization
  • Easier to replicate across projects
  • Supports custom commands and scripts
  • Better for team collaboration
  • Changes tracked in Git history

2.2: Export Your Job Configuration to YAML

SAP CI/CD makes it easy to transition from Job Editor to repository configuration by allowing you to export your current settings. This is a great practice as it gives you a template to start with...

Steps to export configuration:

  1. In SAP Continuous Integration and Delivery, navigate to your job `cicd-service-main`
  2. Go to the Stages tab
  3. Click YML (next to the Job Editor button)
  4. Click Copy to copy the YAML configuration to your clipboard

Screenshot 2.1: Exporting job configuration to YAML

Screenshot 2.2: YAML configuration dialog

2.3: Understanding the Configuration Structure

Build Stage:

stages:
  build:
    buildTool: "mta"                    # Build tool (mta, npm, maven, etc.)
    buildDescriptor: "mta.yaml"         # MTA descriptor file
    buildToolVersion: "MBTJ21N22"       # MBT with Java 21 and Node 22

Malware Scan Stage:

  malwareScan:
    scan: false                 # Enable/disable security scanning

Additional Tests Stage:

  additionalTests:
    npmTests:
      buildDescriptor: "package.json"
      npmScript: "test"        # npm script to run

2.4: Create Configuration File in Repository

Now let's add this configuration to our repository. SAP CI/CD looks for configuration in `.sap_cid/config.yaml`:

Create the configuration directory and file:

# Create directory
mkdir .sap_cid

# Create config file
touch .sap_cid/config.yaml

Add the prior exported configuration to `.sap_cid/config.yaml`.

Screenshot 2.4: config.yaml file in repository

2.5: Switch Job to Source Repository Configuration

Now let's switch our job to use this repository configuration:

  1. In SAP CI/CD, navigate to your job `cicd-service-main`
  2. Click Edit
  3. In the Stages tab, find the Configuration Mode dropdown
  4. Change from Job Editor to Source Repository
  5. Click Save

Screenshot 2.5: Switching to Source Repository configuration mode

Important: Once you switch to Source Repository mode, the Job Editor will not have the configuration steps. All configuration changes must be made in the `.sap_cid/config.yaml` file and committed to Git.

2.6: Commit and Push Configuration

Let's commit our new configuration to Git:

git add .sap_cid/config.yaml
git commit -m "Add Source Repository configuration for CI/CD pipeline"
git push

2.7: Verify Configuration is Applied

Trigger a new build to verify the configuration is being read from the repository:

Screenshot 2.8: Build using Source Repository configuration

2.8: Add Custom Commands to Build Stage

Now let's customize our pipeline by adding a custom command that runs during the build stage. We'll create an npm script and execute it using the `runFirst` command. This demonstrates how to add custom logic while using the MTA build tool.

First, add a custom script to `package.json`:

{
  "name": "cicd-service",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "cds-serve",
    "test": "jest",
    "build": "cds build --production",
    "build-info": "echo '=====================================' && echo 'Build Information' && echo '=====================================' && echo \"Build Date: $(date)\" && echo \"🔧 Node Version: $(node --version)\" && echo \"NPM Version: $(npm --version)\" && echo '=====================================' && echo ''"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@sap/cds": "^7",
    "express": "^4"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "jest": "^29",
    "@sap/cds-dk": "^7"
  }
}

Then, update `.sap_cid/config.yaml` to call this script:

---
stages:
  build:
    buildTool: "mta"
    buildDescriptor: "mta.yaml"
    buildToolVersion: "MBTJ21N22"
    runFirst:
      command: "npm run build-info"
  malwareScan:
    scan: false
  additionalTests:
    npmTests:
      buildDescriptor: "package.json"
      npmScript: "test"

How it works:

  • `runFirst` executes commands at the beginning of the build stage (before MTA build)
  • We call `npm run build-info` which executes our custom script from package.json
  • The script displays useful build information (date, Node version, npm version)
  • Use `&&` to chain multiple commands - each subsequent command only executes if the preceding one succeeds

2.9: Commit Changes and Test

git add .sap_cid/config.yaml package.json
git commit -m "Add custom build-info script to pipeline"
git push

Run the job in CI/CD service.

Screenshot 2.12: Build information output in logs.

2.10: Advanced Configuration Options

Here are some additional configuration options you can explore, I would recommend you to check the following guides for this:

  • Configure a Cloud Foundry Environment Job in Your Repository (Stages Configuration Repository section)
  • Adding Additional Commands to Stages

2.11: Troubleshooting Configuration Issues

Build fails after switching to Source Repository:

  • Verify `.sap_cid/config.yaml` exists in repository root
  • Check YAML syntax (use a YAML validator)
  • Ensure all referenced npm scripts exist in package.json

Configuration changes not applied:

  • Make sure changes are committed and pushed to Git
  • Verify the job is in "Source Repository" mode
  • Check the branch name matches the job configuration

"Configuration file not found" error:

  • File must be named exactly `.sap_cid/config.yaml`
  • File must be in the repository root
  • Case-sensitive on Linux systems

---

Part 3: Webhook Automation & Event-Driven CI/CD

Manual builds are great for learning, but the real power of CI/CD comes from automation. Let's set up webhooks to trigger builds automatically when you push code.

3.1: Understanding Webhooks

Webhooks are HTTP callbacks that Git providers (GitHub, GitLab, etc.) send to SAP CI/CD service when events occur in your repository.

Common webhook events:

  • Push to branch
  • Pull request created/updated
  • Tag created
  • Branch created/deleted

3.2: Create Webhook Credential and Get Webhook Data

Before we can set up the webhook, we need to create a webhook credential in SAP CI/CD and get the webhook information.

Step 1: Navigate to Repository Settings

  1. In SAP Continuous Integration and Delivery, go to the Repositories tab
  2. Click on your repository (`cicd-service`)
  3. The detail view opens with a Webhook Event Receiver section

Step 2: Create Webhook Credential

In the Webhook Event Receiver section:

  1. Type: Select GitHub from the dropdown
  2. Webhook Credential: Open the matchcode, and then click on the + (plus) icon to create a new credential
  3. A dialog opens for creating the credential:
  • Name: Enter a name (e.g., `github-webhook-secret`)
  • Description: Optional description (e.g., "Webhook secret for GitHub integration")
  • Secret: Click Generate to automatically generate a secure secret. IMPORTANT: Copy the generated secret immediately! You'll need it for GitHub and won't be able to see it again
  1. Click Create to save the credential, and click on save the repository we were modifying.

Step 3: Get Webhook Data

After creating the credential, the Webhook Event Receiver section now shows:

  • Payload URL - The webhook URL for GitHub
  • Content Type - `application/json`
  • Webhook Credential - The credential you just created. As said before, now you cannot see it anymore.

Screenshot 3.5: Complete webhook data

Keep this information ready - you'll need it for the next step

3.3: Configure Webhook in GitHub

Now let's configure the webhook in your GitHub repository using the information from SAP CI/CD:

  1. Go to your GitHub repository
  2. Click on the Settings tab (repository settings, not account settings)
  3. From the left navigation pane, choose Webhooks
  4. Click Add webhook

Configure the webhook with the information from SAP CI/CD:

  • Payload URL: Paste the Payload URL from the Webhook Event Receiver section
  • Content type: Select `application/json`
  • Secret: Paste the secret you copied when creating the Webhook Credential
  • Which events would you like to trigger this webhook?
  • Select Just the push event
  • Active: Ensure this checkbox is selected

Screenshot 3.7: Webhook configuration form with SAP CI/CD data

Click Add webhook.

3.4: Test Webhook with a Code Change

Let's make a small change to trigger the webhook:

Update `srv/hello-service.js`:

module.exports = (srv) => {
    srv.on('sayHello', () => {
        return 'Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD! 🚀';
    });
};

Also change the test!

Commit and push:

git add srv/hello-service.js
git commit -m "Add rocket emoji to greeting message"
git push

3.5: Automatic Build Triggered

Within seconds, check your SAP CI/CD service - a new build should automatically start.

Screenshot 3.10: Build automatically triggered by webhook

3.6: Webhook Delivery History

You can verify webhook delivery in GitHub:

  1. Go to your repository's Settings → Webhooks
  2. Click on your webhook
  3. Click Recent Deliveries tab

Screenshot 3.12: Webhook delivery history in GitHub

3.7: Configure Scheduled/Timed Builds

Besides event-driven builds, you can also schedule builds to run periodically (e.g., nightly builds).

In SAP CI/CD job configuration:

  1. Edit your job
  2. Go to Triggers tab
  3. Enable Scheduled Builds
  4. Configure cron pattern

Example cron patterns:

  • 0 2 * * * - Every day at 2 AM
  • 0 0 * * 0 - Every Sunday at midnight
  • 0 */6 * * * - Every 6 hours

Screenshot 3.14: Scheduled build configuration

3.8: Branch-Specific Webhook Triggers

You can configure jobs to only trigger for specific branches:

In Job Configuration:

  • Branch: Use wildcards like `feature/*` or `release/*`
  • Multiple jobs can listen to the same repository on different branches

---

Part 4: Multi-Branch Strategies & Best Practices

Modern development workflows use feature branches for parallel development. Let's configure our CI/CD pipeline to handle multiple branches effectively.

4.1: Understanding Multi-Branch Workflows

Common branching strategies:

GitFlow:

  • `main` - Production code
  • `develop` - Integration branch
  • `feature/*` - Feature development
  • `release/*` - Release preparation
  • `hotfix/*` - Production fixes

GitHub Flow (simpler):

  • `main` - Production code
  • `feature/*` - All development branches

4.2: Create a Feature Branch

Let's create a feature branch for our application:

# Create and switch to feature branch
git checkout -b feature/add-goodbye-endpoint

# Verify current branch
git branch

4.3: Add New Feature to Application

Let's add a new goodbye endpoint to demonstrate multi-branch development:

Update `srv/hello-service.cds`:

service HelloService {
    function sayHello() returns String;
    function sayGoodbye() returns String;
}

Update `srv/hello-service.js`:

module.exports = (srv) => {
    srv.on('sayHello', () => {
        return 'Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD! 🚀';
    });

    srv.on('sayGoodbye', () => {
        return 'Goodbye! Thanks for using SAP Build Code CI/CD! 👋';
    });
};

Add test for new endpoint (`tests/hello-service.test.js`):

const cds = require('@sap/cds/lib');
const path = require('path');

describe('Hello Service', () => {
    let srv;

    beforeAll(async () => {
        const csn = await cds.load(path.join(__dirname, '../srv/hello-service.cds'));
        await cds.deploy(csn);
        srv = await cds.serve('HelloService').from(csn);
    });

    test('sayHello returns correct message', async () => {
        const result = await srv.tx().send('sayHello');
        expect(result).toBe('Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD! 🚀');
    });

    test('sayGoodbye returns correct message', async () => {
        const result = await srv.tx().send('sayGoodbye');
        expect(result).toBe('Goodbye! Thanks for using SAP Build Code CI/CD! 👋');
    });
});

Test locally:

npm test

Screenshot 4.2: Tests passing with new feature

4.4: Commit and Push Feature Branch

git add .
git commit -m "Add goodbye endpoint to HelloService"
git push -u origin feature/add-goodbye-endpoint

Screenshot 4.3: Pushing feature branch

4.5: Create Job for Feature Branches

Now let's create a CI/CD job that automatically builds all feature branches:

  1. In SAP CI/CD, go to Jobs
  2. Click + (Create Job)
  3. Configure:
  • Job Name: `cicd-service-features`
  • Repository: `cicd-service`
  • Branch:`feature/*` (using wildcard)
  • Pipeline: Cloud Foundry Environment
  • Configuration Mode: Repository Configuration

Screenshot 4.4: Creating multi-branch job with wildcard

4.6: Automatic Build for Feature Branch

The webhook automatically triggers a build for the feature branch in the next pushes to remote.

Screenshot 4.6: Feature branch build triggered

4.7: Branch-Specific Configurations

You can customize pipeline behavior per branch pattern. For feature branches, you might want a simpler configuration that skips deployment stages.

4.8: Build Retention Policies

Configure how long to keep build artifacts:

  1. Edit job
  2. Configure Build Retention:
  • Max. Builds: 50 builds
  • Keep Builds for: 7 days

Screenshot 4.9: Build retention configuration

This prevents storage bloat from feature branch builds.

4.9: Multiple Branches in Action

Let's create another feature branch to see multiple parallel builds:

# Create another feature
git checkout main
git checkout -b feature/add-timestamp

# Make a change
echo "// Feature: add timestamp" >> srv/hello-service.js

# Commit and push
git add .
git commit -m "Add timestamp feature placeholder"
git push -u origin feature/add-timestamp

Screenshot 4.10: Multiple feature branches building

4.11: Merge Feature to Main

Once feature is complete and CI passes:

# Switch to main
git checkout main

# Merge feature
git merge feature/add-goodbye-endpoint

# Push to main
git push

This triggers the main branch job automatically.

4.12: Multi-Branch Best Practices

Job Configuration:

  • Separate jobs for `main` vs `feature/*` branches
  • More thorough testing on main branch
  • Skip deployment stages for feature branches
  • Use retention policies to clean up old builds

Branch Strategy:

  • Use consistent naming (feature/*, bugfix/*, hotfix/*)
  • Keep feature branches short-lived
  • Require CI success before merging
  • Delete branches after merging

Configuration:

  • Share common config, override when needed
  • Use branch-specific configs for different behaviors
  • Document branch patterns in a README file

---

Part 5: GitHub Actions Integration - Pull Request Status Checks

While webhooks trigger SAP CI/CD builds automatically, wouldn't it be great if you could see the build status directly in your GitHub Pull Requests? In this section, we'll integrate SAP CI/CD build status with GitHub's PR checks, providing real-time feedback to developers.

5.1: The Integration Challenge

When you create a Pull Request on GitHub, it doesn't automatically know about your SAP CI/CD build status. GitHub shows its own Actions results, but the actual build happening in SAP CI/CD is invisible to the PR workflow. Knowing the status of the tests if they are passing or not, if the image is being built properly or not, if the malware scan detected some anomalous thing, might be interesting prior a merge to main branch with all the code.

What we want to achieve:

  • Automatic build status checks on Pull Requests
  • Block merging if SAP CI/CD build fails
  • Show build URL for easy debugging
  • Wait for build completion and report results

5.2: Enable API Access in SAP BTP

Step 1: Enable API access to SAP CI/CD service. This allows GitHub Actions to query build status programmatically.

Step 2: Create Service Key

  1. Click on your service CI/CD instance
  2. Go to Service Keys tab
  3. Click Create Service Key -> Service Key Name: `cicd-github-actions`
  4. Click Create

Step 3: Copy API Credentials

Click View on your service key to see the credentials. Keep this in hand, we are going to use them for the github integration.

5.3: Configure GitHub Secrets

Now we'll store the API credentials securely in GitHub Secrets.

⚠️ Importnat: GitHub may truncate secrets that contain special characters (or at least this happened to me while configuring this). The `client secret` from the service often contains special characters that can cause authentication failures.

Solution: Base64 encode the client secret before storing it in GitHub.

Step 1: Base64 Encode the Client Secret

# Replace YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET with the actual secret from service key
echo -n "YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET" | base64

Copy the output - this is your base64-encoded secret.

Step 2: Add Secrets to GitHub

  1. Go to your GitHub repository
  2. Click Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions
  3. Click New repository secret

Add the following secrets:

Secret 1: Client ID

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_CLIENT_ID`
  • Value: Your clientid from service key

Secret 2: Client Secret (Base64 Encoded)

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_CLIENT_SECRET_BASE64`
  • Value: The base64-encoded secret from Step 1

Secret 3: Token URL

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_TOKEN_URL`
  • Value: The OAuth URL from service key + `/oauth/token`
  • Example: `https://your-subdomain.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token`

Secret 4: API URL

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_API_URL`
  • Value: The ci/cd service endpoint from service key

Secret 5: Job Name

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_JOB_NAME`
  • Value: Your CI/CD job name (e.g., `cicd-service-main`)

5.4: Create GitHub Actions Workflow

Now let's create a GitHub Actions workflow that checks SAP CI/CD build status for Pull Requests.

Create `.github/workflows/sap-cicd-status.yml`:

name: SAP CI/CD Build Status Check

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened]
    branches:
      - main

jobs:
  check-sap-cicd-build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    name: Check SAP CI/CD Build Status

    steps:
        - name: Get PR Commit SHA
          id: pr-info
          run: |
            echo "commit_sha=${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
            echo "branch=${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
            echo "🔍 PR Commit SHA: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}"
            echo "🌿 Branch: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}"

        - name: Get OAuth Token
          id: get-token
          run: |
            echo "🔐 Getting OAuth token..."

            # Decode base64 secret to handle special characters
            CLIENT_ID="${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_CLIENT_ID }}"
            CLIENT_SECRET=$(echo "${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_CLIENT_SECRET_BASE64 }}" | base64 -d)

            echo "Client ID length: ${#CLIENT_ID}"
            echo "Client Secret length: ${#CLIENT_SECRET}"

            # Use Basic Authentication
            response=$(curl -s -X POST "${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_TOKEN_URL }}" \
              -u "${CLIENT_ID}:${CLIENT_SECRET}" \
              -d "grant_type=client_credentials")

            token=$(echo "$response" | jq -r '.access_token')
            if [ "$token" == "null" ] || [ -z "$token" ]; then
              echo "❌ Failed to get OAuth token"
              echo "Response: $response"
              exit 1
            fi

            echo "✅ OAuth token obtained"
            echo "token=$token" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

        - name: Find Build for Commit
          id: find-build
          run: |
            echo "🔍 Looking for SAP CI/CD build for commit ${{ steps.pr-info.outputs.commit_sha }}..."

            # Try to find the build (with retries in case it hasn't started yet)
            max_attempts=10
            attempt=0
            build_found=false

            while [ $attempt -lt $max_attempts ]; do
              attempt=$((attempt + 1))
              echo "Attempt $attempt/$max_attempts..."

              response=$(curl -s -X GET \
                "${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_API_URL }}/v2/jobs/${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_JOB_NAME }}/builds?filter=latest" \
                -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ steps.get-token.outputs.token }}")

              # Extract commit hash from the latest build
              commit_hash=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0].builtCommit.hash')
              build_status=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0].status')
              build_result=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0].result')
              build_number=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0].number')
              build_url=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0]._links.ui.href')

              echo "Found build #$build_number for commit: $commit_hash"
              echo "Status: $build_status | Result: $build_result"

              if [ "$commit_hash" == "${{ steps.pr-info.outputs.commit_sha }}" ]; then
                echo "✅ Found matching build!"
                build_found=true
                echo "build_status=$build_status" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                echo "build_result=$build_result" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                echo "build_number=$build_number" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                echo "build_url=$build_url" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                break
              fi

              echo "⏳ Build not found yet, waiting 10 seconds..."
              sleep 10
            done

            if [ "$build_found" = false ]; then
              echo "❌ Could not find build for commit ${{ steps.pr-info.outputs.commit_sha }}"
              exit 1
            fi

        - name: Wait for Build Completion
          id: wait-build
          if: steps.find-build.outputs.build_status != 'FINISHED'
          run: |
            echo "⏳ Build is not finished yet. Waiting for completion..."

            max_wait=1800  # 30 minutes
            elapsed=0
            interval=30

            while [ $elapsed -lt $max_wait ]; do
              response=$(curl -s -X GET \
                "${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_API_URL }}/v2/jobs/${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_JOB_NAME }}/builds/${{ steps.find-build.outputs.build_number }}" \
                -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ steps.get-token.outputs.token }}")

              status=$(echo $response | jq -r '.status')
              result=$(echo $response | jq -r '.result')

              echo "Status: $status | Result: $result"

              if [ "$status" == "FINISHED" ]; then
                echo "✅ Build completed!"
                echo "final_status=$status" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                echo "final_result=$result" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                break
              fi

              echo "Still running... (${elapsed}s elapsed)"
              sleep $interval
              elapsed=$((elapsed + interval))
            done

            if [ $elapsed -ge $max_wait ]; then
              echo "⚠️  Timeout waiting for build to complete"
              exit 1
            fi

        - name: Check Build Result
          run: |
            # Use the final result if we waited, otherwise use the found result
            result="${{ steps.wait-build.outputs.final_result }}"
            if [ -z "$result" ] || [ "$result" == "null" ]; then
              result="${{ steps.find-build.outputs.build_result }}"
            fi

            echo "📊 Final Build Result: $result"
            echo "🔗 Build URL: ${{ steps.find-build.outputs.build_url }}"

            # Check for successful build results
            if [ "$result" == "SUCCESS" ] || [ "$result" == "FINISHED" ]; then
              echo "✅ SAP CI/CD build passed!"
              exit 0
            else
              echo "❌ SAP CI/CD build failed with result: $result"
              echo ""
              echo "Please check the build details at:"
              echo "${{ steps.find-build.outputs.build_url }}"
              exit 1
            fi

5.5: How It Works

Let's break down what this workflow does:

Step 1: Get PR Commit SHA

  • Extracts the commit hash from the Pull Request
  • This is used to match against SAP CI/CD builds

Step 2: Get OAuth Token

  • Obtains OAuth access token for API calls

Step 3: Find Build for Commit

  • Polls SAP CI/CD API for the latest build
  • Matches the commit SHA with the PR commit
  • Retries up to 10 times (builds might not start immediately)
  • Extracts build status, result, number, and URL

Step 4: Wait for Build Completion

  • If build is still running, polls every 30 seconds
  • Maximum wait time: 30 minutes
  • Updates when build status becomes "FINISHED"

Step 5: Check Build Result

  • Evaluates the final build result
  • Provides build URL for debugging failures
  • Exits with error code if build fails

5.6: Commit and Test the Workflow

# Add the workflow file
git add .github/workflows/sap-cicd-status.yml

# Commit
git commit -m "Add GitHub Actions integration for SAP CI/CD build status"

# Push to main
git push

5.7: Test with a Pull Request

Let's test the integration by creating a Pull Request:

# Create a feature branch
git checkout -b feature/test-github-integration

# Make a small change
echo "// Test GitHub integration" >> srv/hello-service.js

# Commit and push
git add srv/hello-service.js
git commit -m "Test: GitHub Actions integration"
git push -u origin feature/test-github-integration

Now create a Pull Request on GitHub targeting the `main` branch.

Screenshot 5.6: Pull Request with status check running

5.8: Watch the Magic Happen

The workflow will:

  1. Trigger automatically when PR is created
  2. Wait for SAP CI/CD build to start
  3. Poll build status until completion
  4. Report results back to the PR

Screenshot 5.7: PR status check successful

5.9: Build Failure Scenario

If the SAP CI/CD build fails (e.g., tests fail, build errors), the GitHub status check will fail and block merging.

The workflow provides the build URL so developers can investigate:

❌ SAP CI/CD build failed with result: FAILURE Please check the build details at: https://cicd-service.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/ui/index.html#/builds/123

5.10: Advanced Configurations

- Configure Branch Protection Rules

To enforce build status before merging:

  1. Go to Repository Settings → Branches
  2. Add rule for `main` branch
  3. Enable Require status checks to pass before merging
  4. Select Check SAP CI/CD Build Status

- Customize Timeout and Retry Values

Adjust polling behavior in the workflow:

# In "Find Build for Commit"
step max_attempts=10 # Number of retries to find
build sleep 10 # Seconds between retries

# In "Wait for Build Completion"
step max_wait=1800 # Maximum wait time (30 minutes)
interval=30 # Poll interval (30 seconds)

- Add Status Badges to your README file

Add a status badge showing CI/CD status in your README.md:

[![SAP CI/CD Status](https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/YOUR-REPO/workflows/SAP%20CI%2FCD%20Build%20Status%20Check/badge.svg)](https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/YOUR-REPO/actions)

5.12: Benefits of This Integration

Developer Experience:

  • See build status directly in Pull Requests
  • Block merging of broken code automatically
  • Quick access to build logs via provided URL
  • No need to switch between GitHub and SAP CI/CD UI

Team Benefits:

  • Enforce quality gates before merge
  • Reduce manual review overhead
  • Catch issues earlier in development cycle
  • Maintain code quality standards

DevOps Benefits:

  • Single source of truth for build status
  • Automated enforcement of CI/CD checks
  • Audit trail of all build results
  • Integration with GitHub's native PR workflow

---

Conclusion & Next Steps

Congratulations! You've successfully set up a comprehensive CI/CD pipeline for your SAP CAP application using SAP Build Code's Continuous Integration & Delivery service, complete with GitHub Actions integration!

What We've Accomplished

In this guide, we covered:

  • Part 1: Set up first CI/CD pipeline with a simple CAP application
  • Part 2: Mastered advanced configuration with repository-based config
  • Part 3: Automated builds with webhooks and event-driven triggers
  • Part 4: Implemented multi-branch strategies for parallel development
  • Part 5: Integrated SAP CI/CD build status with GitHub Pull Requests

Personal Thoughts

In today's competitive landscape, speed of delivery isn't just an advantage, it's a necessity. Automation is the key to
staying agile. The more you automate, the faster you can deliver enhancements and new features to your users.

I strongly encourage you to implement this CI/CD setup and evaluate which projects in your portfolio could benefit from
it. The return on investment is substantial, by automating your deployment pipeline, you eliminate significant
operational overhead and free your team to focus on what truly matters: writing correct, secure, and functional code.
Let the CI/CD service handle the heavy lifting of cloud deployments.

SAP's documentation for the Continuous Integration & Delivery service is comprehensive and includes helpful video
tutorials that guide you through the configuration process step-by-step. Getting started is easier than you might
think.

Stop deploying manually. Start shipping smarter. Your future self (and your team) will thank you.

I am thinking about a post of integrating this service with Cloud Transport Management Service in BTP. I will start considering it and working on it.

See you in the next blog!

Nata.

vertimeCategory=&TransactionCurrency=&sap-app-origin-hint=&sap-xapp-state=AS5679D08E05ZMQPZQW0I0ORA6OY36DKIA2876TJ&/C_ProductObjPg('3100007781')/?sap-iapp-state--history=TASIBOTKHPEE19NGUV2ZA0JG9JGZWSYRV5RVB0T7Z

entry.title: Re: Translation issue in spanish for description of App F...
entry.content:
Good day @S0027154449 

Thank you for your question.

This seems to be a translation typo error. You cannot permanently correct standard app titles or descriptions yourself. The best is to log it as an SAP translation defect, so SAP corrects it in ta future delivery.

You can log that error by going to SAP for Me à Cases à Create Case (incident),

include the App ID, language, and both the correct and incorrect text. I suggest you also add a screenshot of the wrong wording and submit the case as a translation/UI text issue for the Fiori Launchpad content. See KBA 2065287 for more details.

 

In the meantime, what you can do now is to either search with the App ID: F2661 or temporarily switch your logon language to English and search with the English name (Find Maintenance Task List and Operation).

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Unit of Measure for Total Capacity in Storage Bin (LS...

Transactions "LS0xN" relate to SAP-WM (Warehouse Management), not SAP-EWM (Extended Warehouse Management), as your Tag indicates ... (So the Tag seems incorrect?)

Assuming you are referring to SAP-WM, please check :

  • By doing a "F1" on the Total Capacity field, it will already shed some light on its purpose : "In this field you define the total capacity for the storage bin. This value must correspond to the capacity usage values that can be defined in the material master or for the storage unit type."
  • Check SAP-Notes and perhaps this "Guided Answer on How to identify inconsistencies in Weights and Capacities in WM" : https://ga.support.sap.com/index.html#/tree/2514/actions/34235

Hope this helps

Nic T.

entry.title: Re: Error ME113 - Valuation Type Required for Split Valua...
entry.content:
Dear @Keratiya_D,

This is standard behavior / known limitation in Public Cloud:

  • For split-valuated materials, SAP requires a Valuation Type in purchasing documents otherwise you get ME113

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/a88c00e369d24c67b24a5f959dffe893.html

  • However the Valuation Type field is not available in the My Purchase Requisitions app and also not in Manage Purchase Requisition Professional in Public Cloud. SAP Note explicitly points this out and gives the workaround

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3634030

Workaround:

1. Use the app Create Purchase Requisition Advanced. This is the SAP-recommended way to create PRs for split-valuated materials and enter/maintain the Valuation Type

2. In the PR item, maintain Valuation Type choose one of the allowed valuation types for that material/plant then save

If users don’t see Create Purchase Requisition Advanced:

  • It’s a role / catalog assignment issue
  • In Public Cloud use Maintain Business Roles to assign the role or the relevant business catalog containing it to the requester role

More details below:

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('ME51N')/S36

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: FI-CA FX Valuation with Trading Partner Info
entry.content:
To add VBUND (Trading Partner Company Code) in the FX document, you need to implement an enhancement in the structures FKK100 or FKK100N.

Within these structures, you will find the customer include structures:

  • CI_ZFKK100

  • CI_ZFKK100N

These includes can be enhanced to add custom fields such as VBUND, which can then be used to derive the trading partner company code in the FX valuation document.

For older implementations, enhancing CI_ZFKK100 was sufficient and worked correctly. However, for newer implementations, you may also need to enhance CI_ZFKK100N.

Recommended approach:

  1. First enhance the include CI_ZFKK100 and add the required fields such as VBUND.

  2. Test the process with new data.

  3. If the field is still not transferred to the FX document, then enhance CI_ZFKK100N as well.

Both CI_ZFKK100 and CI_ZFKK100N are customer include structures designed for such extensions.

Additionally, you may also need to add the field SHKZG (Debit/Credit Indicator) in the same include structure so that the debit/credit information is correctly transferred to the valuation postings.

In structure FKK100N, these CI includes are used to define customer-specific account assignment fields for foreign currency valuation (for example, business place or trading partner). These fields can then be transferred to the valuation postings.

However, note that these fields may not appear in the ALV display under the Single Records and Postings views.

For reference, you can also review the following SAP Notes:

  • SAP Note 2659631

  • SAP Note 3294782

These notes may not contain the exact implementation steps but reference the relevant structures that can help guide the enhancement.

entry.title: Implementing SAP Business Data Cloud and AI with SAP open data ecosystem
entry.content:
Ever had a customer say, “We love our SAP data… but what about everything else?”

That’s exactly where this session comes in.

We’ll dive into how SAP Business Data Cloud, powered by the SAP open data ecosystem, helps you extend data boundaries and activate AI/ML across SAP and non-SAP sources.  The thing is, AI only gets exciting when it’s fed the right data and when teams actually know how to use it.

From delta share scenarios to hands-on data scientist features like notebooks, we’ll connect strategy to execution.  And yes, we’ll also give you a preview of what’s ahead on the AI roadmap.

Think of it as moving from “we store data” to “we create intelligence”.  Big difference.

Let’s build solutions that customers didn’t even realize were possible.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/jul/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00010668.html

entry.title: SAP’s Most Popular Certification Gets a Major Upgrade
entry.content:
March is officially the month of ABAP. SAP’s most popular certification for back-end developers, the Back-End Developer ABAP Cloud exam, is getting a major upgrade. At the same time, the SAP Community developer challenge for ABAP is going live, making this the perfect moment to sharpen your skills and validate your expertise.

 

A Reimagined Certification Experience

The ABAP Cloud certification exam has been redesigned. Moving beyond traditional question-based formats, the new exam introduces a performance-based, open-book experience. Learners now work directly in authentic SAP systems, navigating AI-supported, real-world scenarios that reflect actual project work. The focus is no longer on memorization; it’s about demonstrating practical skills that matter on the job.

 

What Does It Mean for You?

If you’ve been preparing for certification through the SAP Learning site, you’re already familiar with hands-on exercises and scenario-based tasks embedded in the learning journeys. The new exam format simply extends that approach. From the moment you begin your certification, the experience feels familiar because it mirrors how you’ve been learning all along.

Every practical exam, whether system-based or scenario-driven reflects the reality of SAP projects:

  • Timeboxed tasks performed in live SAP systems
  • Realistic customer and project scenarios
  • AI-supported simulations
  • Open-book access to resources such as SAP Help Portal, SAP Learning, SAP Community, and SAP Joule for Consultants (where applicable)
  • A clear emphasis on proving your ability to do the work

This shift represents something bigger: trust. Trust in learners who invest in building real skills and trust from employers who rely on SAP-certified professionals to deliver value from day one.

 

How to Get Certified as Backend Developer for ABAP Cloud

Earning your certification is a structured journey. Whether you are new to ABAP or looking to formalize years of experience, the following resources will help you build a strong foundation and deepen your expertise.

 

Recommended Courses to Build and Deepen Your Skills

Start with the fundamentals and progressively expand your knowledge:

Course: Learning Basic ABAP Programming

Course: Deepening Your ABAP Programming Knowledge

Course: Building Data Models with the ABAP Dictionary and ABAP Core Data Services

Course: Practicing Clean Core Extensibility for SAP S/4HANA Cloud

SAP Certification: Backend Developer - ABAP Cloud

 

Together, these courses prepare you not just to pass the exam but to succeed in real-world ABAP Cloud development. Learn more about the exam details in this community blog by @JenniferHauck.

 

 

Connect Live with SAP Experts and Strengthen Your Exam Readiness

Learning doesn’t have to be a solo experience. If you’re looking for direct interaction, practical tips, and expert guidance, join the upcoming live sessions available through your SAP Learning Hub subscription:

  • Getting Started with ABAP Cloud Development
  • Get Certified: SAP Certified – Back-End Developer ABAP Cloud
  • Stay Certified: Back-End Developer – ABAP Cloud

These sessions offer the opportunity to clarify questions, reinforce key concepts, and gain confidence before taking the exam.

 

Experience Hands-On Learning at SAP CodeJam Events Near You

If you prefer immersive, in-person learning, don’t miss the upcoming SAP CodeJam events. These workshops are designed to provide practical, guided experience with real SAP technologies in a collaborative environment.

Upcoming sessions include:

  • April 7: ABAP AI: Capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers CodeJam (São Paulo, Brazil)
  • April 8: ABAP AI: Capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers CodeJam (Londrina, Brazil)
  • April 23: ABAP Cloud and the RESTful Application Programming Model (Warsaw, Poland)

These events are an excellent opportunity to deepen your understanding, expand your network, and gain hands-on practice. Be sure to explore the full event calendar regularly, as new sessions are added on an ongoing basis.

 

Continue Your Learning with On-Demand Developer Content

Can’t attend live? Explore past Devtoberfest sessions, Tech Bytes, SAP CodeTalks, and more on the official SAP Developers YouTube channel. On-demand content allows you to learn at your own pace while staying connected to the latest innovations in ABAP and SAP development.

March is the perfect time to invest in your ABAP Cloud journey. With a certification experience designed to reflect real project work, you’re not just passing an exam, you’re demonstrating that you’re ready to build, extend, and innovate in the SAP ecosystem.

entry.title: Streamlining Warehouse Operations: What's New in the Manage Outbound Warehouse Activities App
entry.content:
We are rolling out several updates to the Manage Outbound Warehouse Activities App that make daily tasks smoother and more intuitive. These enhancements reflect feedback from our users.  What has changed? 

 

 Streamlined Navigation and Filtering 

 Finding what you need is now easier with several navigation improvements: 

  • New "Open" Tab: Automatically filters the list report page to show only outbound warehouse requests with an "Open" lifecycle status 

 

  • Direct Stock Access: "Open Related" functionality now navigates directly to assigned stock in the Manage Stock app

 

  • Links: The product field now connects directly to the Display Products app 

 

Better Progress Tracking 

 We've completely redesigned how quantities are displayed to give you the full picture: 

  • Progress Overview: Now visible at both header and item levels directly in the object page 
  • Micro Process Flow: Quantities are now displayed in the micro process flow at the item level, making it easier to understand progress at a glance 

 

  • Improved Context: Previously, you only saw the quantity for the current activity, which would drop to zero once completed. Now you can track progress at any stage 

 

 Simplified and Reorganized Interface 

 The app now focuses specifically on outbound deliveries with a cleaner layout: 

  • Simplification: The Related Documents section has been removed from the header and item object page 
  • Reorganized Columns: The main page now highlights business partner links and progress overviews 
  • Restructured Object Page: Each request now displays the planned goods issue date and progress overview at the top, with a simplified item table below 

 

Want to learn more about SAP Logistics Management?

Explore the SAP Logistics Management Online Course 

Visit the SAP Logistics Management Product Page

entry.title: Re: rating gap analysis layout
entry.content:
Hi @user116 

The gap graph that you see on the screenshot corresponds to the legacy experience for Performance Management forms for an EZ-Rater section with the show-gap="true", whereas the other screenshot is the latest experience, where the rating gaps display as numeric on the side-by-side and no longer as a graph. You can hide with show-gap="false".

More information is available here: 

https://help.sap.com/docs/successfactors-performance-and-goals/implementing-and-managing-performance-management/understanding-rating-gaps-in-latest-form-experience

https://help.sap.com/docs/successfactors-performance-and-goals/implementing-and-managing-performance-management/ez-rater-latest-form-experience

Hope this will help, 

Regards, 

Jordi

entry.title: Missing Depreciation Area in Asset Master despite Company Code Configuration
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I am facing an issue in SAP S/4HANA Cloud regarding Asset Accounting configuration and would appreciate some guidance. I am seeing a discrepancy between the depreciation areas configured for a Company Code and the ones that actually appear on the Asset Master data.

The Scenario:

Company Code:  US company code

Asset Class: 1100 (Buildings)

The Issue:
I noticed that Depreciation Area 16 is missing for the US companies.

When I check the configuration app "Define Depreciation Areas in Company Code" for Company Code US, I clearly see that Depreciation Area 16 (Customer-specific use / Ledger 2L) is configured and present.

However, when I go to the "Manage Fixed Assets" app and look at a test asset (Asset Class 1100) created in the exact same Company Code , Depreciation Area 16 is completely missing from the Valuation tab. (I only see areas 1, 32, 90, 91, 92, and 93).

My Question:
Why would a depreciation area be visible in the Company Code configuration but not show up on the actual asset master record?

Is there a secondary configuration step at the Asset Class level (e.g., a "Deactivate" flag for specific areas within Asset Class 1100) that I need to check in S/4HANA Cloud? If so, which specific SSCUI / Configuration Activity should I look at to enable Area 16 for these assets?

Thank you in advance for your help!

entry.title: Re: Instructor Led items not triggering Surveys
entry.content:
Hi @ThaboMaroga,

There could be multiple reasons for the surveys not getting triggered.

  • The Survey associated to the item is not published & is still in draft state.
  • Required for Item Completion at the Survey level is not enabled
  • Required for Item Completion at the Item level is not enabled
  • The necessary settings are not enabled for the Completion Status to trigger the survey

You can refer to the below KBA's that can help you investigate further.

2523676 - Survey is not Assigned to Users after Course Completion

2318365 - Survey is not triggered after completing an Item

entry.title: Re: Item showing in Chinese instead of English
entry.content: In order for the item titles to be displayed correctly in the user preferref language you must make sure that the title is correctly maintained for the item in that locale. You can click on globe icon and see in which locales the Item titles are populated. So, please make sure that the title is populated for the item in the users preferred locale.
